mappings.rationaleAI: Diplomatic history of the 1919 Treaty of Versailles, competing national interests, and the precarious post-war settlement directly address the Paris Peace Conference (A.1.b), establishment of the League of Nations, and the immediate postwar problems (A.1).

Causes, conduct, and consequences of World War I including the Paris Peace Settlement and the legacy of German resentment directly provide context for the immediate postwar problems (A.1) and the diplomatic isolation that followed.

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: The Great Depression, collapse of financial markets, and Roosevelt's New Deal programs directly address the economic and political impact of the 1930s depression (A.4.b) and the New Deal policy in the U.S. (A.4.c).
The Spanish Civil War (1936-1939), Mussolini's intervention, and Franco's alignment with fascism directly address the Popular Front and the Spanish Civil War (A.3.b).

Fascism in Mussolini's Italy and Hitler's Germany, far-right movements, and mass mobilization directly address Italian aggression against Ethiopia (A.3.b), Nazi Germany's establishment and rearmament (A.4.c), and ideologies in World War II (C.1.b).

Hitler's consolidation of power, territorial expansion, and the Nazi regime directly address establishment of Nazi Germany and economic recovery based on rearmament (A.4.c), German conquest of Poland and France (B.1), and the Nazi-Soviet pact (C.1.b.i).

Comprehensive coverage of World War II from German invasion of Poland through Allied victory addresses all aspects of the war section (B): German conquests (B.1), war in Asia and the Pacific (B.2), war in Europe and North Africa (B.3), Axis Powers (B.4), Allied leadership and diplomacy (B.5), and military technology (B.6).
Military history of aerial warfare covering both World Wars addresses the burgeoning of military technology including developments in aircraft (B.6).

Co-evolution of weapons systems and military tactics, including nuclear weapons and information warfare, directly addresses the burgeoning of military technology (B.6) including communications devices, weapons, missiles, and atomic bombs.

Scientific development of atomic and hydrogen bombs, the Hiroshima and Nagasaki bombings, Cold War arms race, and deterrence theory directly address military technology developments (B.6), atomic and hydrogen bombs (C.2.a.i), and the problem of nuclear energy and balance of terror (C.2.f.viii).

Origins in post-war Europe, NATO formation, Vietnam War, superpower rivalry, nuclear deterrence, and proxy wars directly address the postwar years (C.2), beginnings of the Cold War (C.2.c), the continuing Cold War (C.2.f), and the period since 1957 (C.3) including decolonization, detente, and resolution of the German problem.

Soviet history from revolution through Cold War rivalry addresses War Communism and the New Economic Policy (A.4.c), consolidation of Soviet power in eastern Europe and the Iron Curtain (C.2.c.i), and de-Stalinization (C.2.f.iv).
Explores the establishment and evolving role of the UN, peacekeeping operations, and the Security Council discussed in C.2.a and throughout the postwar section.

Diplomatic history from Versailles through wartime summitry (Stalin and Churchill) to the Iraq War directly addresses post-Paris Peace Conference diplomacy (A.1.b), Allied wartime leadership and diplomacy (B.5), and international relations before and during World War II (C.1).
EU institutional structure, evolution from the European Coal and Steel Community, single market, and enlargement directly address integration movements in western Europe (C.3.b.ii).

Transition from colonial empires to independent nation-states, anti-colonial activism, and Nehru as independence leader address colonial empires and demands for self-determination (A.3.c), colonial territories of Asia (C.2.d.i), and decolonization (C.3.a.ii).
Addresses the Palestine question, the founding of Israel, and the Arab-Israeli wars discussed in C.2.e and C.3.c.ii.
The 1991 Gulf War, 2003 invasion, and anti-ISIS campaign directly address the Persian Gulf wars (C.3.c.ii) and Middle Eastern conflict.

Communism's global spread from Russia through China, Eastern Europe, Cuba, and Southeast Asia addresses War Communism and the Soviet New Economic Policy (A.4.c), Soviet consolidation in eastern Europe (C.2.c), civil war in China and communist rule (C.2.d.ii), and the Soviet Union and national liberation (C.3.a.iii).
Terrorism as revolutionary violence, religious extremism, the September 2001 attacks, and ISIS directly address international terrorism (C.3.c.iv).

Japan's militarism, World War II involvement, and Cold War alignment address the rise of the militarists in Japan and the Greater East Asia Co-prosperity Sphere (A.3.a) and the war in Asia and the Pacific (B.2).

Modern China from revolution through economic reform addresses civil conflict in China and Japanese seizure of Manchuria (A.3.a), civil war in China and communist rule (C.2.d.ii), and Sino-Soviet relations (C.3.a.viii).
Mao Zedong's leadership of the Chinese Communist Party, revolution, Great Leap Forward, and Cultural Revolution directly address civil war in China and communist rule (C.2.d.ii).
Nationalism as self-determination ideology connects to increased demands for self-determination among subject peoples (A.3.c) and the new states and nonalignment (C.3.a.iv).
Nuclear weapons proliferation, NATO, state security, Cold War legacy, and the Iraq War address the continuing Cold War (C.2.f) and the Great Powers and the world since 1957 (C.3.a).
International relations as a discipline examining sovereignty, global political change, and theoretical frameworks provides broad context for the entire international relations section (C).
Concentration camps under the Third Reich and the Soviet Gulag connect to the Axis Powers' leadership and political systems (B.4) and Soviet consolidation of power in eastern Europe (C.2.c.i).
Military strategy including annihilation, attrition, and deterrence connects to the strategic plans and tactical procedures of the Axis Powers (B.4) and Allied forces (B.5).

Warfare from the modern period covering the Spanish Civil War, World War II, the Cold War, and guerrilla tactics addresses the Spanish Civil War (A.3.b), World War II (B), the continuing Cold War (C.2.f), and Southeast Asia's Indochina War (C.3.c.i).
Intrastate armed conflicts, their causes and transnational dimensions connect to areas of conflict including Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and sub-Saharan Africa (C.3.c).

European reconstruction, Cold War division, and decolonization address reconstruction and European political instability (C.2.b), beginnings of the Cold War (C.2.c), and peace in postwar Europe and integration movements (C.3.b).

Authoritarian regimes, military and single-party types, and democratization address dictatorships in new nations of central and eastern Europe (A.1.a), Soviet consolidation (C.2.c.i), and eastern Europe under Soviet imperialism (C.3.b.iii).
Forced migration and refugee crises caused by war and political oppression connect to the areas of conflict (C.3.c) in Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and sub-Saharan Africa.

Iranian civilization from ancient Persia through the 1979 Islamic Revolution, Cold War geopolitics, and relations with major powers address the Iranian case (C.2.e.ii) and the Iran-Iraq War (C.3.c.ii).

Geopolitics as strategic analysis of geographic factors shaping political power, Cold War strategy, and the Global War on Terror connect to the continuing Cold War (C.2.f) and the Great Powers and the world since 1957 (C.3.a).

Peace as concept and practice, peacekeeping operations, and the UN Security Council's role connect to the establishment of the League of Nations (A.1.b) and peaceful coexistence and detente (C.3.a.ix).

Modern humanitarianism covering responses to conflicts, disasters, and refugee crises connects to the areas of conflict (C.3.c) in Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and sub-Saharan Africa where humanitarian interventions have been prominent.
Covers War Communism, the New Economic Policy, and the rise of Stalinism — directly relevant to failed attempts at new governments in Europe and Soviet economic recovery based on rearmament.

Covers interwar British social welfare, 1930s foreign policy debates, and wartime transformation — directly relevant to the Locarno-era rapprochement, postwar reconstruction and social welfare, and the Battle of Britain.

Covers U.S. foreign policy including the Cold War, NATO formation, and containment strategy — directly relevant to the transformation of American engagement in Southeast Asia and peaceful coexistence and detente.
Covers the Sino-American rapprochement during the Cultural Revolution and its geopolitical consequences — directly relevant to Sino-Soviet relations and the consequences of their rift.
Covers the Korean peninsula's division and the geopolitical dynamics of the Korean War — directly relevant to the Korean War, its meaning, course, and consequences.
Covers the Great Depression, collapse of the world market, and varied governmental responses — directly relevant to the economic and political impact of the Great Depression.
Examines European colonial empires and decolonization processes — directly relevant to colonial empires and demands for self-determination, and postwar decolonization.
Covers the emergence of BRICS nations challenging Western-dominated institutions — relevant to new states, nonalignment, and domestic problems in post-Cold War international relations.
Covers historical development of welfare state systems from the New Deal era — relevant to increased government control, reconstruction, and social welfare in the 1920s (unresolved leaf A.4.a).
